revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{short description|Ward of Osaka, Japan}} [[File:Sumiyoshi-ku in Osaka City.svg|thumb|Location of Sumiyoshi-ku in Osaka City]] {{Nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi-ku'''|住吉区}} is one of 24 [[:en:Wards of Japan|wards]] of [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is located on the southern part of the [[:en:Uemachi Plateau|Uemachi Plateau]], in the southernmost part of Osaka City, and is separated from [[:en:Sakai City|Sakai City]]'s Sakai-ku and Kita-ku by the Yamato River. There are six rail lines, and three main thoroughfares - Abiko-Suji (which run north–south through the centre of the ward), Abeno-Suji (which continues north through the area of [[:en:Tennoji|Tennoji]]) and Nagai Koen-Dori, which runs east–west and connects the area with the port to the west. The northern part of Sumiyoshi-ku is a residential area which is a continuation of the southern part of [[:en:Abeno-ku, Osaka|Abeno-ku]]. The Tezukayama 1-Chome neighbourhood in Abeno-ku, and Tezukayama-naka and Tezukayama-nishi neighbourhoods in Sumiyoshi-ku are upper-class residential areas. South of this, around the [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine]], are the Sumiyoshi and Kamisumiyoshi neighbourhoods, home to many long established local families. Surrounding these are the middle-class residential neighbourhoods of Shimizugaoka, Suminoe, Oriono, Dairyo, Minamisumiyoshi, Yamanouchi, and Nagai, which lies at the eastern end of the ward. Sumiyoshi-ku is home to about 157,000 residents, and has a population density of 16,800 people per square kilometer.
==Area history== [[File:sumiyoshi-taisya seimon.jpg|thumb|right|[[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]]] In ancient times, the ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'' combination for the current day Sumiyoshi, 住吉, was pronounced ''Suminoe'', and appeared in {{Lang|ja-latn|[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]}} (8th-century Japanese poetry). At present, Sumiyoshi, Suminoe, and Sumie represent different area names.
[[:en:Suminoe no Tsu|Suminoe no Tsu]] (Suminoe Port), which in ancient times was located south of Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine, was home to Japan's first international port, and until Naniwa no Tsu (Naniwa Port) was constructed, it was the country's international point of contact. The Japanese envoy to the [[:en:Sui dynasty|Sui]] and [[:en:Tang dynasty|T'ang]] dynasties of [[:en:China|China]] departed there, and it was also Japan's portal to the [[:en:Silk Road|Silk Road]]. Importantly, [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] was also introduced to Japan (via China and Korea) through this port.
In the Middle Ages, the throne of [[:en:Emperor Go-Murakami|Emperor Go-Murakami]] of the Southern Court was placed in the house of the chief priest of Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine for ten years. It was known as Sumiyoshi-angou (住吉行宮) and was the base of the Southern Court.
There was also a town built around Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine, and until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and ensuing [[:en:Land reclamation|Land reclamation]] projects, the sea shore was quite close to the shrine. ''Sumiyoshi Moyou'', a famous picture map showing a Japanese ''place of scenic beauty'' (風光明媚な風景) depicts the landscape in the area of the shrine at the time.
In ancient times, the estates of a number of powerful family clans were located in the Tezukayama area. Included among these was the house of the famous Ootomo No Kanamura, who supported Emperor Keitai. Tezukayama Kofun is the burial site of either Ootomo No Kanamura or one of his children.
==Ward history== Sumiyoshi-ku (ward) was originally called Sumiyoshi-gun (district) of the [[:en:Settsu province|Settsu province]]. In ancient times the area prospered independently of the central Osaka area.
In 1878, an act was passed for the organization of areas into a county system of ''gun'' (district), ''ku'' (ward), ''machi'' (town) and ''mura'' (village/neighbourhood). From this Sumiyoshi-gun (district) was created. The district town hall was built in the only town of the district, Anryuu-machi. In April 1896, a new county system was introduced which resulted in Sumiyoshi-gun being absorbed into Higashinari-gun.
Sumiyoshi-ku was formed in 1925 with the second expansion of Osaka city into an area that was formerly part of the original Sumiyoshi-gun. In 1943, two sections were separated from Sumiyoshi-ku to form [[:en:Abeno-ku, Osaka|Abeno-ku]] and [[:en:Higashisumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|Higashisumiyoshi-ku]]. Also, in 1943, part of [[:en:Nishinari-ku, Osaka|Nishinari-ku]] and all of Kohamachi-ku were absorbed into Sumiyoshi-ku. In 1974 Sumiyoshi-ku was further subdivided, resulting in [[:en:Sumiyoshi Park|Sumiyoshi Park]], Anryuu, and Hamaguchi becoming part of the newly formed [[:en:Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]].
==Places of interest== Aside from Sumiyoshi Taisha, there is also a large number of restaurants along Abiko-Suji and in the Nagai area (the intersection of Nagai Koen-Dori and Abiko-Suji), as well as numerous small shops and businesses on Abeno-Suji. Nagai also abuts [[:en:Nagai Park|Nagai Park]] in neighboring [[:en:Higashisumiyoshi Ward|Higashisumiyoshi Ward]] which includes [[:en:Nagai Stadium|Nagai Stadium]], [[:en:Nagai Botanical Garden|Nagai Botanical Garden]], as well as the [[:en:Osaka Museum of Natural History|Osaka Museum of Natural History]].
*[[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine]] *Tezukayama Kofun *Ruins of the palace of Emperor Go-Murakami *Ikune Shrine *Oyosami Shrine *Asazawa Shrine *Wakamatsu Shrine (also known as Todorokihimenomikoto Shrine) *Gokurakuji Temple *Shogon Jodoji Temple *Statue of a Guardian Deity, Rokudo-no Tsuji Enma Jizo *Monument to Osho Ikkyu's Hermitage, Shosaian *Kishu Highway *Kumano Road *Abiko Kannon *Mandai-ike Pond *Asaka Central Park *Yamato River *Hosoe River
==Education==
* [[:en:Baekdu Hagwon|Baekdu Hagwon]] * [[:en:Osaka City University|Osaka City University]] * [[Naniwa Junior & Senior High School]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=学校法人浪速学院 浪速高等学校 |url=https://www.naniwa.ed.jp/high/ |access-date=2024-11-03 |website=学校法人浪速学院 浪速高等学校 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Notable people from Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka == *[[:en:Keiji Obiki|Keiji Obiki]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Kenichi Maeyamada|Kenichi Maeyamada]], Japanese composer, lyricist, and musician *[[:en:LaSalle Ishii|LaSalle Ishii]], Japanese TV personality, actor, voice actor, and theater director *[[:en:Mami Yamasaki|Mami Yamasaki]], Japanese gravure idol and actress *[[:en:Masaya Onosaka|Masaya Onosaka]], Japanese voice actor *[[:en:Minoru Niihara|Minoru Niihara]], Japanese singer *[[:en:Mizuki Matsuda|Mizuki Matsuda]], Japanese long-distance runner *[[:en:Naoki Matoba|Naoki Matoba]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Naoko Kawai|Naoko Kawai]], Japanese pop idol, singer-songwriter, and composer *[[:en:Ryosuke Ogata|Ryosuke Ogata]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Takashi Kawai|Takashi Kawai]], Japanese professional baseball player
== References == {{reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons category-inline}} *[http://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/contents/wdu020/sumiyoshi/english/ Official website of Sumiyoshi] {{in lang|en}}

*[[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine]] *Tezukayama Kofun *Ruins of the palace of Emperor Go-Murakami *Ikune Shrine *Oyosami Shrine *Asazawa Shrine *Wakamatsu Shrine (also known as Todorokihimenomikoto Shrine) *Gokurakuji Temple *Shogon Jodoji Temple *Statue of a Guardian Deity, Rokudo-no Tsuji Enma Jizo *Monument to Osho Ikkyu's Hermitage, Shosaian *Kishu Highway *Kumano Road *Abiko Kannon *Mandai-ike Pond *Asaka Central Park *Yamato River *Hosoe River

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q985702]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi-ku [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Ward of Osaka, Japan}} [[File:Sumiyoshi-ku in Osaka City.svg|thumb|Location of Sumiyoshi-ku in Osaka City]] {{Nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi-ku'''|住吉区}} is one of 24 [[:en:Wards of Japan|wards]] of [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is located on the southern part of the [[:en:Uemachi Plateau|Uemachi Plateau]], in the southernmost part of Osaka City, and is separated from [[:en:Sakai City|Sakai City]]'s Sakai-ku and Kita-ku by the Yamato River. There are six rail lines, and three main thoroughfares - Abiko-Suji (which run north–south through the centre of the ward), Abeno-Suji (which continues north through the area of [[:en:Tennoji|Tennoji]]) and Nagai Koen-Dori, which runs east–west and connects the area with the port to the west. The northern part of Sumiyoshi-ku is a residential area which is a continuation of the southern part of [[:en:Abeno-ku, Osaka|Abeno-ku]]. The Tezukayama 1-Chome neighbourhood in Abeno-ku, and Tezukayama-naka and Tezukayama-nishi neighbourhoods in Sumiyoshi-ku are upper-class residential areas. South of this, around the [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine]], are the Sumiyoshi and Kamisumiyoshi neighbourhoods, home to many long established local families. Surrounding these are the middle-class residential neighbourhoods of Shimizugaoka, Suminoe, Oriono, Dairyo, Minamisumiyoshi, Yamanouchi, and Nagai, which lies at the eastern end of the ward. Sumiyoshi-ku is home to about 157,000 residents, and has a population density of 16,800 people per square kilometer.
==Area history== [[File:sumiyoshi-taisya seimon.jpg|thumb|right|[[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]]] In ancient times, the ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'' combination for the current day Sumiyoshi, 住吉, was pronounced ''Suminoe'', and appeared in {{Lang|ja-latn|[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]}} (8th-century Japanese poetry). At present, Sumiyoshi, Suminoe, and Sumie represent different area names.
[[:en:Suminoe no Tsu|Suminoe no Tsu]] (Suminoe Port), which in ancient times was located south of Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine, was home to Japan's first international port, and until Naniwa no Tsu (Naniwa Port) was constructed, it was the country's international point of contact. The Japanese envoy to the [[:en:Sui dynasty|Sui]] and [[:en:Tang dynasty|T'ang]] dynasties of [[:en:China|China]] departed there, and it was also Japan's portal to the [[:en:Silk Road|Silk Road]]. Importantly, [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] was also introduced to Japan (via China and Korea) through this port.
In the Middle Ages, the throne of [[:en:Emperor Go-Murakami|Emperor Go-Murakami]] of the Southern Court was placed in the house of the chief priest of Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine for ten years. It was known as Sumiyoshi-angou (住吉行宮) and was the base of the Southern Court.
There was also a town built around Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine, and until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and ensuing [[:en:Land reclamation|Land reclamation]] projects, the sea shore was quite close to the shrine. ''Sumiyoshi Moyou'', a famous picture map showing a Japanese ''place of scenic beauty'' (風光明媚な風景) depicts the landscape in the area of the shrine at the time.
In ancient times, the estates of a number of powerful family clans were located in the Tezukayama area. Included among these was the house of the famous Ootomo No Kanamura, who supported Emperor Keitai. Tezukayama Kofun is the burial site of either Ootomo No Kanamura or one of his children.
==Ward history== Sumiyoshi-ku (ward) was originally called Sumiyoshi-gun (district) of the [[:en:Settsu province|Settsu province]]. In ancient times the area prospered independently of the central Osaka area.
In 1878, an act was passed for the organization of areas into a county system of ''gun'' (district), ''ku'' (ward), ''machi'' (town) and ''mura'' (village/neighbourhood). From this Sumiyoshi-gun (district) was created. The district town hall was built in the only town of the district, Anryuu-machi. In April 1896, a new county system was introduced which resulted in Sumiyoshi-gun being absorbed into Higashinari-gun.
Sumiyoshi-ku was formed in 1925 with the second expansion of Osaka city into an area that was formerly part of the original Sumiyoshi-gun. In 1943, two sections were separated from Sumiyoshi-ku to form [[:en:Abeno-ku, Osaka|Abeno-ku]] and [[:en:Higashisumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|Higashisumiyoshi-ku]]. Also, in 1943, part of [[:en:Nishinari-ku, Osaka|Nishinari-ku]] and all of Kohamachi-ku were absorbed into Sumiyoshi-ku. In 1974 Sumiyoshi-ku was further subdivided, resulting in [[:en:Sumiyoshi Park|Sumiyoshi Park]], Anryuu, and Hamaguchi becoming part of the newly formed [[:en:Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]].
==Places of interest== Aside from Sumiyoshi Taisha, there is also a large number of restaurants along Abiko-Suji and in the Nagai area (the intersection of Nagai Koen-Dori and Abiko-Suji), as well as numerous small shops and businesses on Abeno-Suji. Nagai also abuts [[:en:Nagai Park|Nagai Park]] in neighboring [[:en:Higashisumiyoshi Ward|Higashisumiyoshi Ward]] which includes [[:en:Nagai Stadium|Nagai Stadium]], [[:en:Nagai Botanical Garden|Nagai Botanical Garden]], as well as the [[:en:Osaka Museum of Natural History|Osaka Museum of Natural History]].
*[[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine]] *Tezukayama Kofun *Ruins of the palace of Emperor Go-Murakami *Ikune Shrine *Oyosami Shrine *Asazawa Shrine *Wakamatsu Shrine (also known as Todorokihimenomikoto Shrine) *Gokurakuji Temple *Shogon Jodoji Temple *Statue of a Guardian Deity, Rokudo-no Tsuji Enma Jizo *Monument to Osho Ikkyu's Hermitage, Shosaian *Kishu Highway *Kumano Road *Abiko Kannon *Mandai-ike Pond *Asaka Central Park *Yamato River *Hosoe River
==Education==
* [[:en:Baekdu Hagwon|Baekdu Hagwon]] * [[:en:Osaka City University|Osaka City University]] * [[Naniwa Junior & Senior High School]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=学校法人浪速学院 浪速高等学校 |url=https://www.naniwa.ed.jp/high/ |access-date=2024-11-03 |website=学校法人浪速学院 浪速高等学校 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Notable people from Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka == *[[:en:Keiji Obiki|Keiji Obiki]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Kenichi Maeyamada|Kenichi Maeyamada]], Japanese composer, lyricist, and musician *[[:en:LaSalle Ishii|LaSalle Ishii]], Japanese TV personality, actor, voice actor, and theater director *[[:en:Mami Yamasaki|Mami Yamasaki]], Japanese gravure idol and actress *[[:en:Masaya Onosaka|Masaya Onosaka]], Japanese voice actor *[[:en:Minoru Niihara|Minoru Niihara]], Japanese singer *[[:en:Mizuki Matsuda|Mizuki Matsuda]], Japanese long-distance runner *[[:en:Naoki Matoba|Naoki Matoba]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Naoko Kawai|Naoko Kawai]], Japanese pop idol, singer-songwriter, and composer *[[:en:Ryosuke Ogata|Ryosuke Ogata]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Takashi Kawai|Takashi Kawai]], Japanese professional baseball player
== References == {{reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons category-inline}} *[http://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/contents/wdu020/sumiyoshi/english/ Official website of Sumiyoshi] {{in lang|en}}

In 1943, two sections were separated from Sumiyoshi-ku to form [[:en:Abeno-ku, Osaka|Abeno-ku]] and [[:en:Higashisumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|Higashisumiyoshi-ku]]. Also, in 1943, part of [[:en:Nishinari-ku, Osaka|Nishinari-ku]] and all of Kohamachi-ku were absorbed into Sumiyoshi-ku. In 1974 Sumiyoshi-ku was further subdivided, resulting in [[:en:Sumiyoshi Park|Sumiyoshi Park]], Anryuu, and Hamaguchi becoming part of the newly formed [[:en:Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]].
==Places of interest== Aside from Sumiyoshi Taisha, there is also a large number of restaurants along Abiko-Suji and in the Nagai area (the intersection of Nagai Koen-Dori and Abiko-Suji), as well as numerous small shops and businesses on Abeno-Suji. Nagai also abuts [[:en:Nagai Park|Nagai Park]] in neighboring [[:en:Higashisumiyoshi Ward|Higashisumiyoshi Ward]] which includes [[:en:Nagai Stadium|Nagai Stadium]], [[:en:Nagai Botanical Garden|Nagai Botanical Garden]], as well as the [[:en:Osaka Museum of Natural History|Osaka Museum of Natural History]].

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:سومييوشي-كو]][[ca:Sumiyoshi]][[en:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]][[eo:Kvartalo Sumijoŝi]][[es:Sumiyoshi-ku (Osaka)]][[fa:سومی‌یوشی، اوساکا]][[fi:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[it:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[ja:住吉区]][[ko:스미요시구]][[pl:Sumiyoshi (Osaka)]][[sco:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]][[th:เขตซูมิโยชิ]][[tr:Sumiyoshi]][[zh-yue:住吉區]][[zh:住吉區]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q985702]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi-ku [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Ward of Osaka, Japan}} [[File:Sumiyoshi-ku in Osaka City.svg|thumb|Location of Sumiyoshi-ku in Osaka City]] {{Nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi-ku'''|住吉区}} is one of 24 [[:en:Wards of Japan|wards]] of [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is located on the southern part of the [[:en:Uemachi Plateau|Uemachi Plateau]], in the southernmost part of Osaka City, and is separated from [[:en:Sakai City|Sakai City]]'s Sakai-ku and Kita-ku by the Yamato River. There are six rail lines, and three main thoroughfares - Abiko-Suji (which run north–south through the centre of the ward), Abeno-Suji (which continues north through the area of [[:en:Tennoji|Tennoji]]) and Nagai Koen-Dori, which runs east–west and connects the area with the port to the west. The northern part of Sumiyoshi-ku is a residential area which is a continuation of the southern part of [[:en:Abeno-ku, Osaka|Abeno-ku]]. The Tezukayama 1-Chome neighbourhood in Abeno-ku, and Tezukayama-naka and Tezukayama-nishi neighbourhoods in Sumiyoshi-ku are upper-class residential areas. South of this, around the [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine]], are the Sumiyoshi and Kamisumiyoshi neighbourhoods, home to many long established local families. Surrounding these are the middle-class residential neighbourhoods of Shimizugaoka, Suminoe, Oriono, Dairyo, Minamisumiyoshi, Yamanouchi, and Nagai, which lies at the eastern end of the ward. Sumiyoshi-ku is home to about 157,000 residents, and has a population density of 16,800 people per square kilometer.
==Area history== [[File:sumiyoshi-taisya seimon.jpg|thumb|right|[[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]]] In ancient times, the ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'' combination for the current day Sumiyoshi, 住吉, was pronounced ''Suminoe'', and appeared in {{Lang|ja-latn|[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]}} (8th-century Japanese poetry). At present, Sumiyoshi, Suminoe, and Sumie represent different area names.
[[:en:Suminoe no Tsu|Suminoe no Tsu]] (Suminoe Port), which in ancient times was located south of Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine, was home to Japan's first international port, and until Naniwa no Tsu (Naniwa Port) was constructed, it was the country's international point of contact. The Japanese envoy to the [[:en:Sui dynasty|Sui]] and [[:en:Tang dynasty|T'ang]] dynasties of [[:en:China|China]] departed there, and it was also Japan's portal to the [[:en:Silk Road|Silk Road]]. Importantly, [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] was also introduced to Japan (via China and Korea) through this port.
In the Middle Ages, the throne of [[:en:Emperor Go-Murakami|Emperor Go-Murakami]] of the Southern Court was placed in the house of the chief priest of Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine for ten years. It was known as Sumiyoshi-angou (住吉行宮) and was the base of the Southern Court.
There was also a town built around Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine, and until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and ensuing [[:en:Land reclamation|Land reclamation]] projects, the sea shore was quite close to the shrine. ''Sumiyoshi Moyou'', a famous picture map showing a Japanese ''place of scenic beauty'' (風光明媚な風景) depicts the landscape in the area of the shrine at the time.
In ancient times, the estates of a number of powerful family clans were located in the Tezukayama area. Included among these was the house of the famous Ootomo No Kanamura, who supported Emperor Keitai. Tezukayama Kofun is the burial site of either Ootomo No Kanamura or one of his children.
==Ward history== Sumiyoshi-ku (ward) was originally called Sumiyoshi-gun (district) of the [[:en:Settsu province|Settsu province]]. In ancient times the area prospered independently of the central Osaka area.
In 1878, an act was passed for the organization of areas into a county system of ''gun'' (district), ''ku'' (ward), ''machi'' (town) and ''mura'' (village/neighbourhood). From this Sumiyoshi-gun (district) was created. The district town hall was built in the only town of the district, Anryuu-machi. In April 1896, a new county system was introduced which resulted in Sumiyoshi-gun being absorbed into Higashinari-gun.
Sumiyoshi-ku was formed in 1925 with the second expansion of Osaka city into an area that was formerly part of the original Sumiyoshi-gun. In 1943, two sections were separated from Sumiyoshi-ku to form [[:en:Abeno-ku, Osaka|Abeno-ku]] and [[:en:Higashisumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|Higashisumiyoshi-ku]]. Also, in 1943, part of [[:en:Nishinari-ku, Osaka|Nishinari-ku]] and all of Kohamachi-ku were absorbed into Sumiyoshi-ku. In 1974 Sumiyoshi-ku was further subdivided, resulting in [[:en:Sumiyoshi Park|Sumiyoshi Park]], Anryuu, and Hamaguchi becoming part of the newly formed [[:en:Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]].
==Places of interest== Aside from Sumiyoshi Taisha, there is also a large number of restaurants along Abiko-Suji and in the Nagai area (the intersection of Nagai Koen-Dori and Abiko-Suji), as well as numerous small shops and businesses on Abeno-Suji. Nagai also abuts [[:en:Nagai Park|Nagai Park]] in neighboring [[:en:Higashisumiyoshi Ward|Higashisumiyoshi Ward]] which includes [[:en:Nagai Stadium|Nagai Stadium]], [[:en:Nagai Botanical Garden|Nagai Botanical Garden]], as well as the [[:en:Osaka Museum of Natural History|Osaka Museum of Natural History]].
*[[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine]] *Tezukayama Kofun *Ruins of the palace of Emperor Go-Murakami *Ikune Shrine *Oyosami Shrine *Asazawa Shrine *Wakamatsu Shrine (also known as Todoroki-hime-no-Mikoto Shrine) *Gokurakuji Temple *Shogon Jodoji Temple *Statue of a Guardian Deity, Rokudo-no Tsuji Enma Jizo *Monument to Osho Ikkyu's Hermitage, Shosaian *Kishu Highway *Kumano Road *Abiko Kannon *Mandai-ike Pond *Asaka Central Park *Yamato River *Hosoe River
==Education==
* [[:en:Baekdu Hagwon|Baekdu Hagwon]] * [[:en:Osaka City University|Osaka City University]] * [[Naniwa Junior & Senior High School]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=学校法人浪速学院 浪速高等学校 |url=https://www.naniwa.ed.jp/high/ |access-date=2024-11-03 |website=学校法人浪速学院 浪速高等学校 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Notable people from Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka == *[[:en:Keiji Obiki|Keiji Obiki]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Kenichi Maeyamada|Kenichi Maeyamada]], Japanese composer, lyricist, and musician *[[:en:LaSalle Ishii|LaSalle Ishii]], Japanese TV personality, actor, voice actor, and theater director *[[:en:Mami Yamasaki|Mami Yamasaki]], Japanese gravure idol and actress *[[:en:Masaya Onosaka|Masaya Onosaka]], Japanese voice actor *[[:en:Minoru Niihara|Minoru Niihara]], Japanese singer *[[:en:Mizuki Matsuda|Mizuki Matsuda]], Japanese long-distance runner *[[:en:Naoki Matoba|Naoki Matoba]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Naoko Kawai|Naoko Kawai]], Japanese pop idol, singer-songwriter, and composer *[[:en:Ryosuke Ogata|Ryosuke Ogata]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Takashi Kawai|Takashi Kawai]], Japanese professional baseball player

==Area history== [[File:sumiyoshi-taisya seimon.jpg|thumb|right|[[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]]] In ancient times, the ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'' combination for the current day Sumiyoshi, 住吉, was pronounced ''Suminoe'', and appeared in {{Lang|ja-latn|[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]}} (8th-century Japanese poetry). At present, Sumiyoshi, Suminoe, and Sumie represent different area names.
[[:en:Suminoe no Tsu|Suminoe no Tsu]] (Suminoe Port), which in ancient times was located south of Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine, was home to Japan's first international port, and until Naniwa no Tsu (Naniwa Port) was constructed, it was the country's international point of contact. The Japanese envoy to the [[:en:Sui dynasty|Sui]] and [[:en:Tang dynasty|T'ang]] dynasties of [[:en:China|China]] departed there, and it was also Japan's portal to the [[:en:Silk Road|Silk Road]]. Importantly, [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] was also introduced to Japan (via China and Korea) through this port.
In the Middle Ages, the throne of [[:en:Emperor Go-Murakami|Emperor Go-Murakami]] of the Southern Court was placed in the house of the chief priest of Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine for ten years. It was known as Sumiyoshi-angou (住吉行宮) and was the base of the Southern Court.
There was also a town built around Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine, and until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and ensuing [[:en:Land reclamation|Land reclamation]] projects, the sea shore was quite close to the shrine. ''Sumiyoshi Moyou'', a famous picture map showing a Japanese ''place of scenic beauty'' (風光明媚な風景) depicts the landscape in the area of the shrine at the time.
In ancient times, the estates of a number of powerful family clans were located in the Tezukayama area. Included among these was the house of the famous Ootomo No Kanamura, who supported Emperor Keitai. Tezukayama Kofun is the burial site of either Ootomo No Kanamura or one of his children.
==Ward history== Sumiyoshi-ku (ward) was originally called Sumiyoshi-gun (district) of the [[:en:Settsu province|Settsu province]]. In ancient times the area prospered independently of the central Osaka area.
In 1878, an act was passed for the organization of areas into a county system of ''gun'' (district), ''ku'' (ward), ''machi'' (town) and ''mura'' (village/neighbourhood). From this Sumiyoshi-gun (district) was created. The district town hall was built in the only town of the district, Anryuu-machi. In April 1896, a new county system was introduced which resulted in Sumiyoshi-gun being absorbed into Higashinari-gun.
Sumiyoshi-ku was formed in 1925 with the second expansion of Osaka city into an area that was formerly part of the original Sumiyoshi-gun. In 1943, two sections were separated from Sumiyoshi-ku to form [[:en:Abeno-ku, Osaka|Abeno-ku]] and [[:en:Higashisumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|Higashisumiyoshi-ku]]. Also, in 1943, part of [[:en:Nishinari-ku, Osaka|Nishinari-ku]] and all of Kohamachi-ku were absorbed into Sumiyoshi-ku. In 1974 Sumiyoshi-ku was further subdivided, resulting in [[:en:Sumiyoshi Park|Sumiyoshi Park]], Anryuu, and Hamaguchi becoming part of the newly formed [[:en:Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]].
==Places of interest== Aside from Sumiyoshi Taisha, there is also a large number of restaurants along Abiko-Suji and in the Nagai area (the intersection of Nagai Koen-Dori and Abiko-Suji), as well as numerous small shops and businesses on Abeno-Suji. Nagai also abuts [[:en:Nagai Park|Nagai Park]] in neighboring [[:en:Higashisumiyoshi Ward|Higashisumiyoshi Ward]] which includes [[:en:Nagai Stadium|Nagai Stadium]], [[:en:Nagai Botanical Garden|Nagai Botanical Garden]], as well as the [[:en:Osaka Museum of Natural History|Osaka Museum of Natural History]].
*[[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine]] *Tezukayama Kofun *Ruins of the palace of Emperor Go-Murakami *Ikune Shrine *Oyosami Shrine *Asazawa Shrine *Wakamatsu Shrine (also known as Todoroki-hime-no-Mikoto Shrine) *Gokurakuji Temple *Shogon Jodoji Temple *Statue of a Guardian Deity, Rokudo-no Tsuji Enma Jizo *Monument to Osho Ikkyu's Hermitage, Shosaian *Kishu Highway *Kumano Road *Abiko Kannon *Mandai-ike Pond *Asaka Central Park *Yamato River *Hosoe River
==Education==
* [[:en:Baekdu Hagwon|Baekdu Hagwon]] * [[:en:Osaka City University|Osaka City University]] * [[Naniwa Junior & Senior High School]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=学校法人浪速学院 浪速高等学校 |url=https://www.naniwa.ed.jp/high/ |access-date=2024-11-03 |website=学校法人浪速学院 浪速高等学校 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Notable people from Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka == *[[:en:Keiji Obiki|Keiji Obiki]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Kenichi Maeyamada|Kenichi Maeyamada]], Japanese composer, lyricist, and musician *[[:en:LaSalle Ishii|LaSalle Ishii]], Japanese TV personality, actor, voice actor, and theater director *[[:en:Mami Yamasaki|Mami Yamasaki]], Japanese gravure idol and actress *[[:en:Masaya Onosaka|Masaya Onosaka]], Japanese voice actor *[[:en:Minoru Niihara|Minoru Niihara]], Japanese singer *[[:en:Mizuki Matsuda|Mizuki Matsuda]], Japanese long-distance runner *[[:en:Naoki Matoba|Naoki Matoba]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Naoko Kawai|Naoko Kawai]], Japanese pop idol, singer-songwriter, and composer *[[:en:Ryosuke Ogata|Ryosuke Ogata]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Takashi Kawai|Takashi Kawai]], Japanese professional baseball player

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:سومييوشي-كو]][[ca:Sumiyoshi]][[en:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]][[eo:Kvartalo Sumijoŝi]][[es:Sumiyoshi-ku (Osaka)]][[fa:سومی‌یوشی، اوساکا]][[fi:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[it:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[ja:住吉区]][[ko:스미요시구]][[pl:Sumiyoshi (Osaka)]][[sco:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]][[th:เขตซูมิโยชิ]][[tr:Sumiyoshi]][[zh-yue:住吉區]][[zh:住吉區]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q985702]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi-ku [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Ward of Osaka, Japan}} [[File:Sumiyoshi-ku in Osaka City.svg|thumb|Location of Sumiyoshi-ku in Osaka City]] {{Nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi-ku'''|住吉区}} is one of 24 [[:en:Wards of Japan|wards]] of [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is located on the southern part of the [[:en:Uemachi Plateau|Uemachi Plateau]], in the southernmost part of Osaka City, and is separated from [[:en:Sakai City|Sakai City]]'s Sakai-ku and Kita-ku by the Yamato River. There are six rail lines, and three main thoroughfares - Abiko-Suji (which run north–south through the centre of the ward), Abeno-Suji (which continues north through the area of [[:en:Tennoji|Tennoji]]) and Nagai Koen-Dori, which runs east–west and connects the area with the port to the west. The northern part of Sumiyoshi-ku is a residential area which is a continuation of the southern part of [[:en:Abeno-ku, Osaka|Abeno-ku]]. The Tezukayama 1-Chome neighbourhood in Abeno-ku, and Tezukayama-naka and Tezukayama-nishi neighbourhoods in Sumiyoshi-ku are upper-class residential areas. South of this, around the [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine]], are the Sumiyoshi and Kamisumiyoshi neighbourhoods, home to many long established local families. Surrounding these are the middle-class residential neighbourhoods of Shimizugaoka, Suminoe, Oriono, Dairyo, Minamisumiyoshi, Yamanouchi, and Nagai, which lies at the eastern end of the ward. Sumiyoshi-ku is home to about 157,000 residents, and has a population density of 16,800 people per square kilometer.

The Japanese envoy to the [[:en:Sui dynasty|Sui]] and [[:en:Tang dynasty|T'ang]] dynasties of [[:en:China|China]] departed there, and it was also Japan's portal to the [[:en:Silk Road|Silk Road]]. Importantly, [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] was also introduced to Japan (via China and Korea) through this port.
In the Middle Ages, the throne of [[:en:Emperor Go-Murakami|Emperor Go-Murakami]] of the Southern Court was placed in the house of the chief priest of Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine for ten years. It was known as Sumiyoshi-angou (住吉行宮) and was the base of the Southern Court.
There was also a town built around Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine, and until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and ensuing [[:en:Land reclamation|Land reclamation]] projects, the sea shore was quite close to the shrine. ''Sumiyoshi Moyou'', a famous picture map showing a Japanese ''place of scenic beauty'' (風光明媚な風景) depicts the landscape in the area of the shrine at the time.
In ancient times, the estates of a number of powerful family clans were located in the Tezukayama area. Included among these was the house of the famous Ootomo No Kanamura, who supported Emperor Keitai. Tezukayama Kofun is the burial site of either Ootomo No Kanamura or one of his children.
==Ward history== Sumiyoshi-ku (ward) was originally called Sumiyoshi-gun (district) of the [[:en:Settsu province|Settsu province]]. In ancient times the area prospered independently of the central Osaka area.
In 1878, an act was passed for the organization of areas into a county system of ''gun'' (district), ''ku'' (ward), ''machi'' (town) and ''mura'' (village/neighbourhood). From this Sumiyoshi-gun (district) was created. The district town hall was built in the only town of the district, Anryuu-machi. In April 1896, a new county system was introduced which resulted in Sumiyoshi-gun being absorbed into Higashinari-gun.
Sumiyoshi-ku was formed in 1925 with the second expansion of Osaka city into an area that was formerly part of the original Sumiyoshi-gun. In 1943, two sections were separated from Sumiyoshi-ku to form [[:en:Abeno-ku, Osaka|Abeno-ku]] and [[:en:Higashisumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|Higashisumiyoshi-ku]]. Also, in 1943, part of [[:en:Nishinari-ku, Osaka|Nishinari-ku]] and all of Kohamachi-ku were absorbed into Sumiyoshi-ku. In 1974 Sumiyoshi-ku was further subdivided, resulting in [[:en:Sumiyoshi Park|Sumiyoshi Park]], Anryuu, and Hamaguchi becoming part of the newly formed [[:en:Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]].
==Places of interest== Aside from Sumiyoshi Taisha, there is also a large number of restaurants along Abiko-Suji and in the Nagai area (the intersection of Nagai Koen-Dori and Abiko-Suji), as well as numerous small shops and businesses on Abeno-Suji. Nagai also abuts [[:en:Nagai Park|Nagai Park]] in neighboring [[:en:Higashisumiyoshi Ward|Higashisumiyoshi Ward]] which includes [[:en:Nagai Stadium|Nagai Stadium]], [[:en:Nagai Botanical Garden|Nagai Botanical Garden]], as well as the [[:en:Osaka Museum of Natural History|Osaka Museum of Natural History]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:سومييوشي-كو]][[ca:Sumiyoshi]][[en:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]][[eo:Kvartalo Sumijoŝi]][[es:Sumiyoshi-ku (Osaka)]][[fa:سومی‌یوشی، اوساکا]][[fi:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[it:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[ja:住吉区]][[ko:스미요시구]][[pl:Sumiyoshi (Osaka)]][[sco:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]][[th:เขตซูมิโยชิ]][[tr:Sumiyoshi]][[zh-yue:住吉區]][[zh:住吉區]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q985702]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi-ku [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Ward of Osaka, Japan}} [[File:Sumiyoshi-ku in Osaka City.svg|thumb|Location of Sumiyoshi-ku in Osaka City]] {{Nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi-ku'''|住吉区}} is one of 24 [[:en:Wards of Japan|wards]] of [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is located on the southern part of the [[:en:Uemachi Plateau|Uemachi Plateau]], in the southernmost part of Osaka City, and is separated from [[:en:Sakai City|Sakai City]]'s Sakai-ku and Kita-ku by the Yamato River. There are six rail lines, and three main thoroughfares - Abiko-Suji (which run north–south through the centre of the ward), Abeno-Suji (which continues north through the area of [[:en:Tennoji|Tennoji]]) and Nagai Koen-Dori, which runs east–west and connects the area with the port to the west. The northern part of Sumiyoshi-ku is a residential area which is a continuation of the southern part of [[:en:Abeno-ku, Osaka|Abeno-ku]]. The Tezukayama 1-Chome neighbourhood in Abeno-ku, and Tezukayama-naka and Tezukayama-nishi neighbourhoods in Sumiyoshi-ku are upper-class residential areas. South of this, around the [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine]], are the Sumiyoshi and Kamisumiyoshi neighbourhoods, home to many long established local families. Surrounding these are the middle-class residential neighbourhoods of Shimizugaoka, Suminoe, Oriono, Dairyo, Minamisumiyoshi, Yamanouchi, and Nagai, which lies at the eastern end of the ward. Sumiyoshi-ku is home to about 157,000 residents, and has a population density of 16,800 people per square kilometer.
==Area history== [[File:sumiyoshi-taisya seimon.jpg|thumb|right|[[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]]] In ancient times, the ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'' combination for the current day Sumiyoshi, 住吉, was pronounced ''Suminoe'', and appeared in {{Lang|ja-latn|[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]}} (8th-century Japanese poetry). At present, Sumiyoshi, Suminoe, and Sumie represent different area names.
[[:en:Suminoe no Tsu|Suminoe no Tsu]] (Suminoe Port), which in ancient times was located south of Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine, was home to Japan's first international port, and until Naniwa no Tsu (Naniwa Port) was constructed, it was the country's international point of contact. The Japanese envoy to the [[:en:Sui dynasty|Sui]] and [[:en:Tang dynasty|T'ang]] dynasties of [[:en:China|China]] departed there, and it was also Japan's portal to the [[:en:Silk Road|Silk Road]]. Importantly, [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] was also introduced to Japan (via China and Korea) through this port.
In the Middle Ages, the throne of [[:en:Emperor Go-Murakami|Emperor Go-Murakami]] of the Southern Court was placed in the house of the chief priest of Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine for ten years. It was known as Sumiyoshi-angou (住吉行宮) and was the base of the Southern Court.
There was also a town built around Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine, and until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and ensuing [[:en:Land reclamation|Land reclamation]] projects, the sea shore was quite close to the shrine. ''Sumiyoshi Moyou'', a famous picture map showing a Japanese ''place of scenic beauty'' (風光明媚な風景) depicts the landscape in the area of the shrine at the time.
In ancient times, the estates of a number of powerful family clans were located in the Tezukayama area. Included among these was the house of the famous Ootomo No Kanamura, who supported Emperor Keitai. Tezukayama Kofun is the burial site of either Ootomo No Kanamura or one of his children.
==Ward history== Sumiyoshi-ku (ward) was originally called Sumiyoshi-gun (district) of the [[:en:Settsu province|Settsu province]]. In ancient times the area prospered independently of the central Osaka area.
In 1878, an act was passed for the organization of areas into a county system of ''gun'' (district), ''ku'' (ward), ''machi'' (town) and ''mura'' (village/neighbourhood). From this Sumiyoshi-gun (district) was created. The district town hall was built in the only town of the district, Anryuu-machi. In April 1896, a new county system was introduced which resulted in Sumiyoshi-gun being absorbed into Higashinari-gun.
Sumiyoshi-ku was formed in 1925 with the second expansion of Osaka city into an area that was formerly part of the original Sumiyoshi-gun. In 1943, two sections were separated from Sumiyoshi-ku to form [[:en:Abeno-ku, Osaka|Abeno-ku]] and [[:en:Higashisumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|Higashisumiyoshi-ku]]. Also, in 1943, part of [[:en:Nishinari-ku, Osaka|Nishinari-ku]] and all of Kohamachi-ku were absorbed into Sumiyoshi-ku. In 1974 Sumiyoshi-ku was further subdivided, resulting in [[:en:Sumiyoshi Park|Sumiyoshi Park]], Anryuu, and Hamaguchi becoming part of the newly formed [[:en:Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]].
==Places of interest== Aside from Sumiyoshi Taisha, there is also a large number of restaurants along Abiko-Suji and in the Nagai area (the intersection of Nagai Koen-Dori and Abiko-Suji), as well as numerous small shops and businesses on Abeno-Suji. Nagai also abuts [[:en:Nagai Park|Nagai Park]] in neighboring [[:en:Higashisumiyoshi Ward|Higashisumiyoshi Ward]] which includes [[:en:Nagai Stadium|Nagai Stadium]], [[:en:Nagai Botanical Garden|Nagai Botanical Garden]], as well as the [[:en:Osaka Museum of Natural History|Osaka Museum of Natural History]].
*[[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine]] *Tezukayama Kofun *Ruins of the palace of Emperor Go-Murakami *Ikune Shrine *Oyosami Shrine *Asazawa Shrine *Wakamatsu Shrine (also known as Todoroki-hime-no-Mikoto Shrine) *Gokurakuji Temple *Shogon Jodoji Temple *Statue of a Guardian Deity, Rokudo-no Tsuji Enma Jizo *Monument to Osho Ikkyu's Hermitage, Shosaian *Kishu Highway *Kumano Road *Abiko Kannon *Mandai-ike Pond *Asaka Central Park *Yamato River *Hosoe River
==Education==
* [[:en:Baekdu Hagwon|Baekdu Hagwon]] * [[:en:Osaka City University|Osaka City University]] * [[Naniwa Junior & Senior High School]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=学校法人浪速学院 浪速高等学校 |url=https://www.naniwa.ed.jp/high/ |access-date=2024-11-03 |website=学校法人浪速学院 浪速高等学校 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Notable people from Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka == *[[:en:Keiji Obiki|Keiji Obiki]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Kenichi Maeyamada|Kenichi Maeyamada]], Japanese composer, lyricist, and musician *[[:en:LaSalle Ishii|LaSalle Ishii]], Japanese TV personality, actor, voice actor, and theater director *[[:en:Mami Yamasaki|Mami Yamasaki]], Japanese gravure idol and actress *[[:en:Masaya Onosaka|Masaya Onosaka]], Japanese voice actor *[[:en:Minoru Niihara|Minoru Niihara]], Japanese singer *[[:en:Mizuki Matsuda|Mizuki Matsuda]], Japanese long-distance runner *[[:en:Naoki Matoba|Naoki Matoba]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Naoko Kawai|Naoko Kawai]], Japanese pop idol, singer-songwriter, and composer *[[:en:Ryosuke Ogata|Ryosuke Ogata]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Takashi Kawai|Takashi Kawai]], Japanese professional baseball player

*[[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine]] *Tezukayama Kofun *Ruins of the palace of Emperor Go-Murakami *Ikune Shrine *Oyosami Shrine *Asazawa Shrine *Wakamatsu Shrine (also known as Todoroki-hime-no-Mikoto Shrine) *Gokurakuji Temple *Shogon Jodoji Temple *Statue of a Guardian Deity, Rokudo-no Tsuji Enma Jizo *Monument to Osho Ikkyu's Hermitage, Shosaian *Kishu Highway *Kumano Road *Abiko Kannon *Mandai-ike Pond *Asaka Central Park *Yamato River *Hosoe River
==Education==
* [[:en:Baekdu Hagwon|Baekdu Hagwon]] * [[:en:Osaka City University|Osaka City University]] * [[Naniwa Junior & Senior High School]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=学校法人浪速学院 浪速高等学校 |url=https://www.naniwa.ed.jp/high/ |access-date=2024-11-03 |website=学校法人浪速学院 浪速高等学校 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Notable people from Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka == *[[:en:Keiji Obiki|Keiji Obiki]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Kenichi Maeyamada|Kenichi Maeyamada]], Japanese composer, lyricist, and musician *[[:en:LaSalle Ishii|LaSalle Ishii]], Japanese TV personality, actor, voice actor, and theater director *[[:en:Mami Yamasaki|Mami Yamasaki]], Japanese gravure idol and actress *[[:en:Masaya Onosaka|Masaya Onosaka]], Japanese voice actor *[[:en:Minoru Niihara|Minoru Niihara]], Japanese singer *[[:en:Mizuki Matsuda|Mizuki Matsuda]], Japanese long-distance runner *[[:en:Naoki Matoba|Naoki Matoba]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Naoko Kawai|Naoko Kawai]], Japanese pop idol, singer-songwriter, and composer *[[:en:Ryosuke Ogata|Ryosuke Ogata]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Takashi Kawai|Takashi Kawai]], Japanese professional baseball player

<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ar:سومييوشي-كو]] [[ca:Sumiyoshi]] [[eo:Kvartalo Sumijoŝi]] [[es:Sumiyoshi-ku (Osaka)]] [[fa:سومی‌یوشی، اوساکا]] [[fi:Sumiyoshi-ku]] [[fr:Sumiyoshi-ku]] [[it:Sumiyoshi-ku]] [[ja:住吉区]] [[ko:스미요시구]] [[pl:Sumiyoshi (Osaka)]] [[sco:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]] [[th:เขตซูมิโยชิ]] [[tr:Sumiyoshi]] [[zh:住吉區]] [[zh-yue:住吉區]] {{wikidata link|Q985702}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:سومييوشي-كو]][[ca:Sumiyoshi]][[en:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]][[eo:Kvartalo Sumijoŝi]][[es:Sumiyoshi-ku (Osaka)]][[fa:سومی‌یوشی، اوساکا]][[fi:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[it:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[ja:住吉区]][[ko:스미요시구]][[pl:Sumiyoshi (Osaka)]][[sco:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]][[th:เขตซูมิโยชิ]][[tr:Sumiyoshi]][[zh-yue:住吉區]][[zh:住吉區]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q985702]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi-ku [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Ward of Osaka, Japan}} [[File:Sumiyoshi-ku in Osaka City.svg|thumb|Location of Sumiyoshi-ku in Osaka City]] {{Nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi-ku'''|住吉区}} is one of 24 [[:en:Wards of Japan|wards]] of [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is located on the southern part of the [[:en:Uemachi Plateau|Uemachi Plateau]], in the southernmost part of Osaka City, and is separated from [[:en:Sakai City|Sakai City]]'s Sakai-ku and Kita-ku by the Yamato River. There are six rail lines, and three main thoroughfares - Abiko-Suji (which run north–south through the centre of the ward), Abeno-Suji (which continues north through the area of [[:en:Tennoji|Tennoji]]) and Nagai Koen-Dori, which runs east–west and connects the area with the port to the west. The northern part of Sumiyoshi-ku is a residential area which is a continuation of the southern part of [[:en:Abeno-ku, Osaka|Abeno-ku]]. The Tezukayama 1-Chome neighbourhood in Abeno-ku, and Tezukayama-naka and Tezukayama-nishi neighbourhoods in Sumiyoshi-ku are upper-class residential areas. South of this, around the [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine]], are the Sumiyoshi and Kamisumiyoshi neighbourhoods, home to many long established local families. Surrounding these are the middle-class residential neighbourhoods of Shimizugaoka, Suminoe, Oriono, Dairyo, Minamisumiyoshi, Yamanouchi, and Nagai, which lies at the eastern end of the ward. Sumiyoshi-ku is home to about 157,000 residents, and has a population density of 16,800 people per square kilometer.
==Area history== [[File:sumiyoshi-taisya seimon.jpg|thumb|right|[[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]]] In ancient times, the ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'' combination for the current day Sumiyoshi, 住吉, was pronounced ''Suminoe'', and appeared in {{Lang|ja-latn|[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]}} (8th-century Japanese poetry). At present, Sumiyoshi, Suminoe, and Sumie represent different area names.
[[:en:Suminoe no Tsu|Suminoe no Tsu]] (Suminoe Port), which in ancient times was located south of Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine, was home to Japan's first international port, and until Naniwa no Tsu (Naniwa Port) was constructed, it was the country's international point of contact. The Japanese envoy to the [[:en:Sui dynasty|Sui]] and [[:en:Tang dynasty|T'ang]] dynasties of [[:en:China|China]] departed there, and it was also Japan's portal to the [[:en:Silk Road|Silk Road]]. Importantly, [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] was also introduced to Japan (via China and Korea) through this port.
In the Middle Ages, the throne of [[:en:Emperor Go-Murakami|Emperor Go-Murakami]] of the Southern Court was placed in the house of the chief priest of Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine for ten years. It was known as Sumiyoshi-angou (住吉行宮) and was the base of the Southern Court.
There was also a town built around Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine, and until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and ensuing [[:en:Land reclamation|Land reclamation]] projects, the sea shore was quite close to the shrine. ''Sumiyoshi Moyou'', a famous picture map showing a Japanese ''place of scenic beauty'' (風光明媚な風景) depicts the landscape in the area of the shrine at the time.
In ancient times, the estates of a number of powerful family clans were located in the Tezukayama area. Included among these was the house of the famous Ootomo No Kanamura, who supported Emperor Keitai. Tezukayama Kofun is the burial site of either Ootomo No Kanamura or one of his children.
==Ward history== Sumiyoshi-ku (ward) was originally called Sumiyoshi-gun (district) of the [[:en:Settsu province|Settsu province]]. In ancient times the area prospered independently of the central Osaka area.
In 1878, an act was passed for the organization of areas into a county system of ''gun'' (district), ''ku'' (ward), ''machi'' (town) and ''mura'' (village/neighbourhood). From this Sumiyoshi-gun (district) was created. The district town hall was built in the only town of the district, Anryuu-machi. In April 1896, a new county system was introduced which resulted in Sumiyoshi-gun being absorbed into Higashinari-gun.
Sumiyoshi-ku was formed in 1925 with the second expansion of Osaka city into an area that was formerly part of the original Sumiyoshi-gun. In 1943, two sections were separated from Sumiyoshi-ku to form [[:en:Abeno-ku, Osaka|Abeno-ku]] and [[:en:Higashisumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|Higashisumiyoshi-ku]]. Also, in 1943, part of [[:en:Nishinari-ku, Osaka|Nishinari-ku]] and all of Kohamachi-ku were absorbed into Sumiyoshi-ku. In 1974 Sumiyoshi-ku was further subdivided, resulting in [[:en:Sumiyoshi Park|Sumiyoshi Park]], Anryuu, and Hamaguchi becoming part of the newly formed [[:en:Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]].
==Places of interest== Aside from Sumiyoshi Taisha, there is also a large number of restaurants along Abiko-Suji and in the Nagai area (the intersection of Nagai Koen-Dori and Abiko-Suji), as well as numerous small shops and businesses on Abeno-Suji. Nagai also abuts [[:en:Nagai Park|Nagai Park]] in neighboring [[:en:Higashisumiyoshi Ward|Higashisumiyoshi Ward]] which includes [[:en:Nagai Stadium|Nagai Stadium]], [[:en:Nagai Botanical Garden|Nagai Botanical Garden]], as well as the [[:en:Osaka Museum of Natural History|Osaka Museum of Natural History]].
*[[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine]] *Tezukayama Kofun *Ruins of the palace of Emperor Go-Murakami *Ikune Shrine *Oyosami Shrine *Asazawa Shrine *Wakamatsu Shrine (also known as Todoroki-hime-no-Mikoto Shrine) *Gokurakuji Temple *Shogon Jodoji Temple *Statue of a Guardian Deity, Rokudo-no Tsuji Enma Jizo *Monument to Osho Ikkyu's Hermitage, Shosaian *Kishu Highway *Kumano Road *Abiko Kannon *Mandai-ike Pond *Asaka Central Park *Yamato River *Hosoe River
==Education==
* [[:en:Baekdu Hagwon|Baekdu Hagwon]] * [[:en:Osaka City University|Osaka City University]] * [[Naniwa Junior & Senior High School]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=学校法人浪速学院 浪速高等学校 |url=https://www.naniwa.ed.jp/high/ |access-date=2024-11-03 |website=学校法人浪速学院 浪速高等学校 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Notable people from Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka == *[[:en:Keiji Obiki|Keiji Obiki]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Kenichi Maeyamada|Kenichi Maeyamada]], Japanese composer, lyricist, and musician *[[:en:LaSalle Ishii|LaSalle Ishii]], Japanese TV personality, actor, voice actor, and theater director *[[:en:Mami Yamasaki|Mami Yamasaki]], Japanese gravure idol and actress *[[:en:Masaya Onosaka|Masaya Onosaka]], Japanese voice actor *[[:en:Minoru Niihara|Minoru Niihara]], Japanese singer *[[:en:Mizuki Matsuda|Mizuki Matsuda]], Japanese long-distance runner *[[:en:Naoki Matoba|Naoki Matoba]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Naoko Kawai|Naoko Kawai]], Japanese pop idol, singer-songwriter, and composer *[[:en:Ryosuke Ogata|Ryosuke Ogata]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Takashi Kawai|Takashi Kawai]], Japanese professional baseball player

==Area history== [[File:sumiyoshi-taisya seimon.jpg|thumb|right|[[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]]] In ancient times, the ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'' combination for the current day Sumiyoshi, 住吉, was pronounced ''Suminoe'', and appeared in {{Lang|ja-latn|[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]}} (8th-century Japanese poetry). At present, Sumiyoshi, Suminoe, and Sumie represent different area names.
[[:en:Suminoe no Tsu|Suminoe no Tsu]] (Suminoe Port), which in ancient times was located south of Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine, was home to Japan's first international port, and until Naniwa no Tsu (Naniwa Port) was constructed, it was the country's international point of contact. The Japanese envoy to the [[:en:Sui dynasty|Sui]] and [[:en:Tang dynasty|T'ang]] dynasties of [[:en:China|China]] departed there, and it was also Japan's portal to the [[:en:Silk Road|Silk Road]]. Importantly, [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] was also introduced to Japan (via China and Korea) through this port.
In the Middle Ages, the throne of [[:en:Emperor Go-Murakami|Emperor Go-Murakami]] of the Southern Court was placed in the house of the chief priest of Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine for ten years. It was known as Sumiyoshi-angou (住吉行宮) and was the base of the Southern Court.
There was also a town built around Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine, and until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and ensuing [[:en:Land reclamation|Land reclamation]] projects, the sea shore was quite close to the shrine. ''Sumiyoshi Moyou'', a famous picture map showing a Japanese ''place of scenic beauty'' (風光明媚な風景) depicts the landscape in the area of the shrine at the time.
In ancient times, the estates of a number of powerful family clans were located in the Tezukayama area. Included among these was the house of the famous Ootomo No Kanamura, who supported Emperor Keitai. Tezukayama Kofun is the burial site of either Ootomo No Kanamura or one of his children.
==Ward history== Sumiyoshi-ku (ward) was originally called Sumiyoshi-gun (district) of the [[:en:Settsu province|Settsu province]]. In ancient times the area prospered independently of the central Osaka area.
In 1878, an act was passed for the organization of areas into a county system of ''gun'' (district), ''ku'' (ward), ''machi'' (town) and ''mura'' (village/neighbourhood). From this Sumiyoshi-gun (district) was created. The district town hall was built in the only town of the district, Anryuu-machi. In April 1896, a new county system was introduced which resulted in Sumiyoshi-gun being absorbed into Higashinari-gun.
Sumiyoshi-ku was formed in 1925 with the second expansion of Osaka city into an area that was formerly part of the original Sumiyoshi-gun. In 1943, two sections were separated from Sumiyoshi-ku to form [[:en:Abeno-ku, Osaka|Abeno-ku]] and [[:en:Higashisumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|Higashisumiyoshi-ku]]. Also, in 1943, part of [[:en:Nishinari-ku, Osaka|Nishinari-ku]] and all of Kohamachi-ku were absorbed into Sumiyoshi-ku. In 1974 Sumiyoshi-ku was further subdivided, resulting in [[:en:Sumiyoshi Park|Sumiyoshi Park]], Anryuu, and Hamaguchi becoming part of the newly formed [[:en:Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]].
==Places of interest== Aside from Sumiyoshi Taisha, there is also a large number of restaurants along Abiko-Suji and in the Nagai area (the intersection of Nagai Koen-Dori and Abiko-Suji), as well as numerous small shops and businesses on Abeno-Suji. Nagai also abuts [[:en:Nagai Park|Nagai Park]] in neighboring [[:en:Higashisumiyoshi Ward|Higashisumiyoshi Ward]] which includes [[:en:Nagai Stadium|Nagai Stadium]], [[:en:Nagai Botanical Garden|Nagai Botanical Garden]], as well as the [[:en:Osaka Museum of Natural History|Osaka Museum of Natural History]].

It is located on the southern part of the [[:en:Uemachi Plateau|Uemachi Plateau]], in the southernmost part of Osaka City, and is separated from [[:en:Sakai City|Sakai City]]'s Sakai-ku and Kita-ku by the Yamato River. There are six rail lines, and three main thoroughfares - Abiko-Suji (which run north–south through the centre of the ward), Abeno-Suji (which continues north through the area of [[:en:Tennoji|Tennoji]]) and Nagai Koen-Dori, which runs east–west and connects the area with the port to the west. The northern part of Sumiyoshi-ku is a residential area which is a continuation of the southern part of [[:en:Abeno-ku, Osaka|Abeno-ku]]. The Tezukayama 1-Chome neighbourhood in Abeno-ku, and Tezukayama-naka and Tezukayama-nishi neighbourhoods in Sumiyoshi-ku are upper-class residential areas. South of this, around the [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine]], are the Sumiyoshi and Kamisumiyoshi neighbourhoods, home to many long established local families. Surrounding these are the middle-class residential neighbourhoods of Shimizugaoka, Suminoe, Oriono, Dairyo, Minamisumiyoshi, Yamanouchi, and Nagai, which lies at the eastern end of the ward. Sumiyoshi-ku is home to about 157,000 residents, and has a population density of 16,800 people per square kilometer.

Also, in 1943, part of [[:en:Nishinari-ku, Osaka|Nishinari-ku]] and all of Kohamachi-ku were absorbed into Sumiyoshi-ku. Nagai also abuts [[:en:Nagai Park|Nagai Park]] in neighboring [[:en:Higashisumiyoshi Ward|Higashisumiyoshi Ward]] which includes [[:en:Nagai Stadium|Nagai Stadium]], [[:en:Nagai Botanical Garden|Nagai Botanical Garden]], as well as the [[:en:Osaka Museum of Natural History|Osaka Museum of Natural History]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:سومييوشي-كو]][[ca:Sumiyoshi]][[en:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]][[eo:Kvartalo Sumijoŝi]][[es:Sumiyoshi-ku (Osaka)]][[fa:سومی‌یوشی، اوساکا]][[fi:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[it:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[ja:住吉区]][[ko:스미요시구]][[pl:Sumiyoshi (Osaka)]][[sco:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]][[th:เขตซูมิโยชิ]][[tr:Sumiyoshi]][[zh_yue:住吉區]][[zh:住吉區]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:سومييوشي-كو]][[ca:Sumiyoshi]][[en:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]][[eo:Kvartalo Sumijoŝi]][[es:Sumiyoshi-ku (Osaka)]][[fa:سومی‌یوشی، اوساکا]][[fi:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[it:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[ja:住吉区]][[ko:스미요시구]][[pl:Sumiyoshi (Osaka)]][[sco:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]][[th:เขตซูมิโยชิ]][[tr:Sumiyoshi]][[zh_yue:住吉區]][[zh:住吉區]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:سومييوشي-كو]][[ca:Sumiyoshi]][[en:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]][[eo:Kvartalo Sumijoŝi]][[es:Sumiyoshi-ku (Osaka)]][[fa:سومی‌یوشی، اوساکا]][[fi:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[it:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[ja:住吉区]][[ko:스미요시구]][[pl:Sumiyoshi (Osaka)]][[sco:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]][[th:เขตซูมิโยชิ]][[tr:Sumiyoshi]][[zh-yue:住吉區]][[zh:住吉區]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q985702]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi-ku [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Ward of Osaka, Japan}} [[File:Sumiyoshi-ku in Osaka City.svg|thumb|Location of Sumiyoshi-ku in Osaka City]] {{Nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi-ku'''|住吉区}} is one of 24 [[:en:Wards of Japan|wards]] of [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is located on the southern part of the [[:en:Uemachi Plateau|Uemachi Plateau]], in the southernmost part of Osaka City, and is separated from [[:en:Sakai City|Sakai City]]'s Sakai-ku and Kita-ku by the Yamato River. There are six rail lines, and three main thoroughfares - Abiko-Suji (which run north–south through the centre of the ward), Abeno-Suji (which continues north through the area of [[:en:Tennoji|Tennoji]]) and Nagai Koen-Dori, which runs east–west and connects the area with the port to the west. The northern part of Sumiyoshi-ku is a residential area which is a continuation of the southern part of [[:en:Abeno-ku, Osaka|Abeno-ku]]. The Tezukayama 1-Chome neighbourhood in Abeno-ku, and Tezukayama-naka and Tezukayama-nishi neighbourhoods in Sumiyoshi-ku are upper-class residential areas. South of this, around the [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine]], are the Sumiyoshi and Kamisumiyoshi neighbourhoods, home to many long established local families. Surrounding these are the middle-class residential neighbourhoods of Shimizugaoka, Suminoe, Oriono, Dairyo, Minamisumiyoshi, Yamanouchi, and Nagai, which lies at the eastern end of the ward. Sumiyoshi-ku is home to about 157,000 residents, and has a population density of 16,800 people per square kilometer.
==Area history== [[File:sumiyoshi-taisya seimon.jpg|thumb|right|[[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]]] In ancient times, the ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'' combination for the current day Sumiyoshi, 住吉, was pronounced ''Suminoe'', and appeared in {{Lang|ja-latn|[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]}} (8th-century Japanese poetry). At present, Sumiyoshi, Suminoe, and Sumie represent different area names.
[[:en:Suminoe no Tsu|Suminoe no Tsu]] (Suminoe Port), which in ancient times was located south of Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine, was home to Japan's first international port, and until Naniwa no Tsu (Naniwa Port) was constructed, it was the country's international point of contact. The Japanese envoy to the [[:en:Sui dynasty|Sui]] and [[:en:Tang dynasty|T'ang]] dynasties of [[:en:China|China]] departed there, and it was also Japan's portal to the [[:en:Silk Road|Silk Road]]. Importantly, [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] was also introduced to Japan (via China and Korea) through this port.
In the Middle Ages, the throne of [[:en:Emperor Go-Murakami|Emperor Go-Murakami]] of the Southern Court was placed in the house of the chief priest of Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine for ten years. It was known as Sumiyoshi-angou (住吉行宮) and was the base of the Southern Court.
There was also a town built around Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine, and until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and ensuing [[:en:Land reclamation|Land reclamation]] projects, the sea shore was quite close to the shrine. ''Sumiyoshi Moyou'', a famous picture map showing a Japanese ''place of scenic beauty'' (風光明媚な風景) depicts the landscape in the area of the shrine at the time.
In ancient times, the estates of a number of powerful family clans were located in the Tezukayama area. Included among these was the house of the famous Ootomo No Kanamura, who supported Emperor Keitai. Tezukayama Kofun is the burial site of either Ootomo No Kanamura or one of his children.
==Ward history== Sumiyoshi-ku (ward) was originally called Sumiyoshi-gun (district) of the [[:en:Settsu province|Settsu province]]. In ancient times the area prospered independently of the central Osaka area.
In 1878, an act was passed for the organization of areas into a county system of ''gun'' (district), ''ku'' (ward), ''machi'' (town) and ''mura'' (village/neighbourhood). From this Sumiyoshi-gun (district) was created. The district town hall was built in the only town of the district, Anryuu-machi. In April 1896, a new county system was introduced which resulted in Sumiyoshi-gun being absorbed into Higashinari-gun.
Sumiyoshi-ku was formed in 1925 with the second expansion of Osaka city into an area that was formerly part of the original Sumiyoshi-gun. In 1943, two sections were separated from Sumiyoshi-ku to form [[:en:Abeno-ku, Osaka|Abeno-ku]] and [[:en:Higashisumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|Higashisumiyoshi-ku]]. Also, in 1943, part of [[:en:Nishinari-ku, Osaka|Nishinari-ku]] and all of Kohamachi-ku were absorbed into Sumiyoshi-ku. In 1974 Sumiyoshi-ku was further subdivided, resulting in [[:en:Sumiyoshi Park|Sumiyoshi Park]], Anryuu, and Hamaguchi becoming part of the newly formed [[:en:Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]].
==Places of interest== Aside from Sumiyoshi Taisha, there is also a large number of restaurants along Abiko-Suji and in the Nagai area (the intersection of Nagai Koen-Dori and Abiko-Suji), as well as numerous small shops and businesses on Abeno-Suji. Nagai also abuts [[:en:Nagai Park|Nagai Park]] in neighboring [[:en:Higashisumiyoshi Ward|Higashisumiyoshi Ward]] which includes [[:en:Nagai Stadium|Nagai Stadium]], [[:en:Nagai Botanical Garden|Nagai Botanical Garden]], as well as the [[:en:Osaka Museum of Natural History|Osaka Museum of Natural History]].
*[[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine]] *Tezukayama Kofun *Ruins of the palace of Emperor Go-Murakami *Ikune Shrine *Oyosami Shrine *Asazawa Shrine *Wakamatsu Shrine (also known as Todoroki-hime-no-Mikoto Shrine) *Gokurakuji Temple *Shogon Jodoji Temple *Statue of a Guardian Deity, Rokudo-no Tsuji Enma Jizo *Monument to Osho Ikkyu's Hermitage, Shosaian *Kishu Highway *Kumano Road *Abiko Kannon *Mandai-ike Pond *Asaka Central Park *Yamato River *Hosoe River
==Education==
* [[:en:Baekdu Hagwon|Baekdu Hagwon]] * [[:en:Osaka City University|Osaka City University]] * [[Naniwa Junior & Senior High School]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=学校法人浪速学院 浪速高等学校 |url=https://www.naniwa.ed.jp/high/ |access-date=2024-11-03 |website=学校法人浪速学院 浪速高等学校 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Notable people from Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka == *[[:en:Keiji Obiki|Keiji Obiki]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Kenichi Maeyamada|Kenichi Maeyamada]], Japanese composer, lyricist, and musician *[[:en:LaSalle Ishii|LaSalle Ishii]], Japanese TV personality, actor, voice actor, and theater director *[[:en:Mami Yamasaki|Mami Yamasaki]], Japanese gravure idol and actress *[[:en:Masaya Onosaka|Masaya Onosaka]], Japanese voice actor *[[:en:Minoru Niihara|Minoru Niihara]], Japanese singer *[[:en:Mizuki Matsuda|Mizuki Matsuda]], Japanese long-distance runner *[[:en:Naoki Matoba|Naoki Matoba]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Naoko Kawai|Naoko Kawai]], Japanese pop idol, singer-songwriter, and composer *[[:en:Ryosuke Ogata|Ryosuke Ogata]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Takashi Kawai|Takashi Kawai]], Japanese professional baseball player

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:سومييوشي-كو]][[ca:Sumiyoshi]][[en:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]][[eo:Kvartalo Sumijoŝi]][[es:Sumiyoshi-ku (Osaka)]][[fa:سومی‌یوشی، اوساکا]][[fi:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[it:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[ja:住吉区]][[ko:스미요시구]][[pl:Sumiyoshi (Osaka)]][[sco:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]][[th:เขตซูมิโยชิ]][[tr:Sumiyoshi]][[zh_yue:住吉區]][[zh:住吉區]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:سومييوشي-كو]][[ca:Sumiyoshi]][[en:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]][[eo:Kvartalo Sumijoŝi]][[es:Sumiyoshi-ku (Osaka)]][[fa:سومی‌یوشی، اوساکا]][[fi:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[it:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[ja:住吉区]][[ko:스미요시구]][[pl:Sumiyoshi (Osaka)]][[sco:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]][[th:เขตซูมิโยชิ]][[tr:Sumiyoshi]][[zh_yue:住吉區]][[zh:住吉區]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:سومييوشي-كو]][[ca:Sumiyoshi]][[en:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]][[eo:Kvartalo Sumijoŝi]][[es:Sumiyoshi-ku (Osaka)]][[fa:سومی‌یوشی، اوساکا]][[fi:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[fr:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[it:Sumiyoshi-ku]][[ja:住吉区]][[ko:스미요시구]][[pl:Sumiyoshi (Osaka)]][[sco:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]][[th:เขตซูมิโยชิ]][[tr:Sumiyoshi]][[zh-yue:住吉區]][[zh:住吉區]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q985702]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi-ku [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Ward of Osaka, Japan}} [[File:Sumiyoshi-ku in Osaka City.svg|thumb|Location of Sumiyoshi-ku in Osaka City]] {{Nihongo|'''Sumiyoshi-ku'''|住吉区}} is one of 24 [[:en:Wards of Japan|wards]] of [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is located on the southern part of the [[:en:Uemachi Plateau|Uemachi Plateau]], in the southernmost part of Osaka City, and is separated from [[:en:Sakai City|Sakai City]]'s Sakai-ku and Kita-ku by the Yamato River. There are six rail lines, and three main thoroughfares - Abiko-Suji (which run north–south through the centre of the ward), Abeno-Suji (which continues north through the area of [[:en:Tennoji|Tennoji]]) and Nagai Koen-Dori, which runs east–west and connects the area with the port to the west. The northern part of Sumiyoshi-ku is a residential area which is a continuation of the southern part of [[:en:Abeno-ku, Osaka|Abeno-ku]]. The Tezukayama 1-Chome neighbourhood in Abeno-ku, and Tezukayama-naka and Tezukayama-nishi neighbourhoods in Sumiyoshi-ku are upper-class residential areas. South of this, around the [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine]], are the Sumiyoshi and Kamisumiyoshi neighbourhoods, home to many long established local families. Surrounding these are the middle-class residential neighbourhoods of Shimizugaoka, Suminoe, Oriono, Dairyo, Minamisumiyoshi, Yamanouchi, and Nagai, which lies at the eastern end of the ward. Sumiyoshi-ku is home to about 157,000 residents, and has a population density of 16,800 people per square kilometer.
==Area history== [[File:sumiyoshi-taisya seimon.jpg|thumb|right|[[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]]] In ancient times, the ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'' combination for the current day Sumiyoshi, 住吉, was pronounced ''Suminoe'', and appeared in {{Lang|ja-latn|[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]}} (8th-century Japanese poetry). At present, Sumiyoshi, Suminoe, and Sumie represent different area names.
[[:en:Suminoe no Tsu|Suminoe no Tsu]] (Suminoe Port), which in ancient times was located south of Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine, was home to Japan's first international port, and until Naniwa no Tsu (Naniwa Port) was constructed, it was the country's international point of contact. The Japanese envoy to the [[:en:Sui dynasty|Sui]] and [[:en:Tang dynasty|T'ang]] dynasties of [[:en:China|China]] departed there, and it was also Japan's portal to the [[:en:Silk Road|Silk Road]]. Importantly, [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] was also introduced to Japan (via China and Korea) through this port.
In the Middle Ages, the throne of [[:en:Emperor Go-Murakami|Emperor Go-Murakami]] of the Southern Court was placed in the house of the chief priest of Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine for ten years. It was known as Sumiyoshi-angou (住吉行宮) and was the base of the Southern Court.
There was also a town built around Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine, and until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] and ensuing [[:en:Land reclamation|Land reclamation]] projects, the sea shore was quite close to the shrine. ''Sumiyoshi Moyou'', a famous picture map showing a Japanese ''place of scenic beauty'' (風光明媚な風景) depicts the landscape in the area of the shrine at the time.
In ancient times, the estates of a number of powerful family clans were located in the Tezukayama area. Included among these was the house of the famous Ootomo No Kanamura, who supported Emperor Keitai. Tezukayama Kofun is the burial site of either Ootomo No Kanamura or one of his children.
==Ward history== Sumiyoshi-ku (ward) was originally called Sumiyoshi-gun (district) of the [[:en:Settsu province|Settsu province]]. In ancient times the area prospered independently of the central Osaka area.
In 1878, an act was passed for the organization of areas into a county system of ''gun'' (district), ''ku'' (ward), ''machi'' (town) and ''mura'' (village/neighbourhood). From this Sumiyoshi-gun (district) was created. The district town hall was built in the only town of the district, Anryuu-machi. In April 1896, a new county system was introduced which resulted in Sumiyoshi-gun being absorbed into Higashinari-gun.
Sumiyoshi-ku was formed in 1925 with the second expansion of Osaka city into an area that was formerly part of the original Sumiyoshi-gun. In 1943, two sections were separated from Sumiyoshi-ku to form [[:en:Abeno-ku, Osaka|Abeno-ku]] and [[:en:Higashisumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|Higashisumiyoshi-ku]]. Also, in 1943, part of [[:en:Nishinari-ku, Osaka|Nishinari-ku]] and all of Kohamachi-ku were absorbed into Sumiyoshi-ku. In 1974 Sumiyoshi-ku was further subdivided, resulting in [[:en:Sumiyoshi Park|Sumiyoshi Park]], Anryuu, and Hamaguchi becoming part of the newly formed [[:en:Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]].
==Places of interest== Aside from Sumiyoshi Taisha, there is also a large number of restaurants along Abiko-Suji and in the Nagai area (the intersection of Nagai Koen-Dori and Abiko-Suji), as well as numerous small shops and businesses on Abeno-Suji. Nagai also abuts [[:en:Nagai Park|Nagai Park]] in neighboring [[:en:Higashisumiyoshi Ward|Higashisumiyoshi Ward]] which includes [[:en:Nagai Stadium|Nagai Stadium]], [[:en:Nagai Botanical Garden|Nagai Botanical Garden]], as well as the [[:en:Osaka Museum of Natural History|Osaka Museum of Natural History]].
*[[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine]] *Tezukayama Kofun *Ruins of the palace of Emperor Go-Murakami *Ikune Shrine *Oyosami Shrine *Asazawa Shrine *Wakamatsu Shrine (also known as Todoroki-hime-no-Mikoto Shrine) *Gokurakuji Temple *Shogon Jodoji Temple *Statue of a Guardian Deity, Rokudo-no Tsuji Enma Jizo *Monument to Osho Ikkyu's Hermitage, Shosaian *Kishu Highway *Kumano Road *Abiko Kannon *Mandai-ike Pond *Asaka Central Park *Yamato River *Hosoe River
==Education==
* [[:en:Baekdu Hagwon|Baekdu Hagwon]] * [[:en:Osaka City University|Osaka City University]] * [[Naniwa Junior & Senior High School]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=学校法人浪速学院 浪速高等学校 |url=https://www.naniwa.ed.jp/high/ |access-date=2024-11-03 |website=学校法人浪速学院 浪速高等学校 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Notable people from Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka == *[[:en:Keiji Obiki|Keiji Obiki]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Kenichi Maeyamada|Kenichi Maeyamada]], Japanese composer, lyricist, and musician *[[:en:LaSalle Ishii|LaSalle Ishii]], Japanese TV personality, actor, voice actor, and theater director *[[:en:Mami Yamasaki|Mami Yamasaki]], Japanese gravure idol and actress *[[:en:Masaya Onosaka|Masaya Onosaka]], Japanese voice actor *[[:en:Minoru Niihara|Minoru Niihara]], Japanese singer *[[:en:Mizuki Matsuda|Mizuki Matsuda]], Japanese long-distance runner *[[:en:Naoki Matoba|Naoki Matoba]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Naoko Kawai|Naoko Kawai]], Japanese pop idol, singer-songwriter, and composer *[[:en:Ryosuke Ogata|Ryosuke Ogata]], Japanese professional baseball player *[[:en:Takashi Kawai|Takashi Kawai]], Japanese professional baseball player
== References == {{reflist}}

Surrounding these are the middle-class residential neighbourhoods of Shimizugaoka, Suminoe, Oriono, Dairyo, Minamisumiyoshi, Yamanouchi, and Nagai, which lies at the eastern end of the ward. ==Area history== [[File:sumiyoshi-taisya seimon.jpg|thumb|right|[[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]]] In ancient times, the ''{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji}}'' combination for the current day Sumiyoshi, 住吉, was pronounced ''Suminoe'', and appeared in {{Lang|ja-latn|{{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū}}}} (8th-century Japanese poetry). At present, Sumiyoshi, Suminoe, and Sumie represent different area names.
{{ill|Suminoe no Tsu|en|Suminoe no Tsu|WD=Q3503628|lt=Suminoe no Tsu}} (Suminoe Port), which in ancient times was located south of Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine, was home to Japan's first international port, and until Naniwa no Tsu (Naniwa Port) was constructed, it was the country's international point of contact. The Japanese envoy to the {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|WD=Q7405|lt=Sui}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=T'ang}} dynasties of {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China}} departed there, and it was also Japan's portal to the {{ill|Silk Road|en|Silk Road|WD=Q36288|lt=Silk Road}}. Importantly, {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} was also introduced to Japan (via China and Korea) through this port.
In the Middle Ages, the throne of {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|Emperor Go-Murakami|WD=Q440447|lt=Emperor Go-Murakami}} of the Southern Court was placed in the house of the chief priest of Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine for ten years. It was known as Sumiyoshi-angou (住吉行宮) and was the base of the Southern Court.
There was also a town built around Sumiyoshi Grand Shrine, and until the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} and ensuing {{ill|Land reclamation|en|Land reclamation|WD=Q1130322|lt=Land reclamation}} projects, the sea shore was quite close to the shrine. ''Sumiyoshi Moyou'', a famous picture map showing a Japanese ''place of scenic beauty'' (風光明媚な風景) depicts the landscape in the area of the shrine at the time.
In ancient times, the estates of a number of powerful family clans were located in the Tezukayama area. Included among these was the house of the famous Ootomo No Kanamura, who supported Emperor Keitai. Tezukayama Kofun is the burial site of either Ootomo No Kanamura or one of his children.
==Ward history== Sumiyoshi-ku (ward) was originally called Sumiyoshi-gun (district) of the [[:en:Settsu province|Settsu province]]. In ancient times the area prospered independently of the central Osaka area.
In 1878, an act was passed for the organization of areas into a county system of ''gun'' (district), ''ku'' (ward), ''machi'' (town) and ''mura'' (village/neighbourhood). From this Sumiyoshi-gun (district) was created. The district town hall was built in the only town of the district, Anryuu-machi. In April 1896, a new county system was introduced which resulted in Sumiyoshi-gun being absorbed into Higashinari-gun.
Sumiyoshi-ku was formed in 1925 with the second expansion of Osaka city into an area that was formerly part of the original Sumiyoshi-gun. In 1943, two sections were separated from Sumiyoshi-ku to form {{ill|Abeno-ku, Osaka|en|Abeno-ku, Osaka|WD=Q489009|lt=Abeno-ku}} and {{ill|Higashisumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|en|Higashisumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|WD=Q861264|lt=Higashisumiyoshi-ku}}. Also, in 1943, part of {{ill|Nishinari-ku, Osaka|en|Nishinari-ku, Osaka|WD=Q985695|lt=Nishinari-ku}} and all of Kohamachi-ku were absorbed into Sumiyoshi-ku. In 1974 Sumiyoshi-ku was further subdivided, resulting in {{ill|Sumiyoshi Park|en|Sumiyoshi Park|WD=Q7637168|lt=Sumiyoshi Park}}, Anryuu, and Hamaguchi becoming part of the newly formed {{ill|Suminoe-ku, Osaka|en|Suminoe-ku, Osaka|WD=Q985711|lt=Suminoe-ku}}.
==Places of interest== Aside from Sumiyoshi Taisha, there is also a large number of restaurants along Abiko-Suji and in the Nagai area (the intersection of Nagai Koen-Dori and Abiko-Suji), as well as numerous small shops and businesses on Abeno-Suji. Nagai also abuts {{ill|Nagai Park|en|Nagai Park|WD=Q5359309|lt=Nagai Park}} in neighboring [[:en:Higashisumiyoshi Ward|Higashisumiyoshi Ward]] which includes {{ill|Nagai Stadium|en|Nagai Stadium|WD=Q494832|lt=Nagai Stadium}}, {{ill|Nagai Botanical Garden|en|Nagai Botanical Garden|WD=Q6149771|lt=Nagai Botanical Garden}}, as well as the {{ill|Osaka Museum of Natural History|en|Osaka Museum of Natural History|WD=Q11440688|lt=Osaka Museum of Natural History}}.
